comment_text.sn9.3:1.1: Kassapagotta appears also in AN 3.91, where he initially resents the training before repenting.
comment_text.sn9.3:1.2: Cheta (variants ceṭa, ceta) is unsatisfactory. Commentary explains as “deer hunter”, apparently from the root chid (“cut, destroy”; cf. Sanskrit chettṛ). A “nimrod” is both a “hunter” and an “idiot”.
comment_text.sn9.3:2.3: Read bhikkhu here as vocative.

root_text.sn9.3:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ āyasmā kassapagotto kosalesu viharati aññatarasmiṁ vanasaṇḍe.
root_text.sn9.3:1.2: Tena kho pana samayena āyasmā kassapagotto divāvihāragato aññataraṁ chetaṁ ovadati.
root_text.sn9.3:1.3: Atha kho yā tasmiṁ vanasaṇḍe adhivatthā devatā āyasmantaṁ kassapagottaṁ saṁvejetukāmā yenāyasmā kassapagotto tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā āyasmantaṁ kassapagottaṁ gāthāhi ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn9.3:5.1: Atha kho āyasmā kassapagotto tāya devatāya saṁvejito saṁvegamāpādīti.

translation_text.sn9.3:1.1: At one time Venerable Kassapagotta was staying in the land of the Kosalans in a certain forest grove.
translation_text.sn9.3:1.2: Now at that time Venerable Kassapagotta, having withdrawn for his day’s meditation, tried to advise a nimrod.
translation_text.sn9.3:1.3: Then the deity haunting that forest approached Kassapagotta wanting to stir him up, and recited these verses:
translation_text.sn9.3:2.3: It’s a waste of time to advise him, mendicant;
translation_text.sn9.3:2.4: he strikes me as an idiot.
translation_text.sn9.3:4.1: Even if you lit ten lamps
translation_text.sn9.3:4.2: and brought them to him, Kassapa,
translation_text.sn9.3:4.4: for he has no eyes to see.”
translation_text.sn9.3:5.1: Impelled by that deity, Venerable Kassapagotta was struck with a sense of urgency.
